title: S4.3 Fleet rollout + demo acceptance (FLAGGED)
description: Cross-build (deploy/openwrt/build.sh); batch update ALL 4 nodes via mjolnir-apply in one rollout (mixed-fleet window means old nodes NXDOMAIN .mesh — do not straddle the demo); live validation: phone joins SSID -> hello.mesh loads zero-config; meshd publish wiki --port N on node A -> wiki.mesh loads from a client on node B within 60s (PRD Demo acceptance metric). Deadline: before 2026-07-06.
design: DEMO RUNBOOK items (from PRD risks): validate DHCP option 114 affordance on representative iOS/Android devices ahead of time (best-effort, not load-bearing — a 'hello.mesh' sign suffices); Android Private DNS (DoT) bypasses the node resolver entirely — demo devices should have it off, document for attendees; Firefox DoH covered by the canary; offline-owner names black-hole until 99f (known limitation, non-blocking for a short demo). Health-gate: S1.4's dnsmasq change rides mjolnir-apply rollback; ethernet 192.168.1.1 is recovery of last resort. test_tier: thorough.

title: S4.2 Multi-node integration: convergence + partition conflict (FLAGGED)
description: Simulated >=2-node harness on the in-memory GossipTransport: publish on A resolves on B within one tick; partition double-claim heals to the identical winner on every node, zero split-brain (NFR7); tombstone propagation <=1 anti-entropy cycle; loser UX state correct on the losing node.
design: WHY FLAGGED (large): automates two PRD metrics ('Conflict resolution correctness', 'Convergence bound'). Reuse the existing mock-transport pattern from crdt sync tests; simulate partition by withholding delivery, then heal and assert convergence + identical answer sets on all nodes. Include the revive case (unpublish then re-publish same owner) across nodes. test_tier: thorough. complexity: large.

title: S4.1 Resolver conformance suite (FLAGGED)
description: Automated dig-class matrix against the embedded responder: well-known A (claimed + pre-claim 192.168.1.1), service A, non-A NODATA on existing names, unknown NXDOMAIN, SRV/TXT construction, SOA fields (D-005), oversized/EDNS0 queries, malformed packets. Runs in cargo test.
design: WHY FLAGGED: this suite IS the PRD 'Resolver correctness' metric (100% pass). Enumerate every answer-discipline case from the PRD as one test each; include the AAAA-on-existing-name NODATA case explicitly (the classic NXDOMAIN-poisons-A bug). test_tier: thorough. ACs: FR1-FR8 covered as a matrix.

title: S3.3 status: names, owners, active conflicts
description: Extend status output: every known name with owner node-id; active conflicts with winner/loser; lost names. dbv discipline: explicit none marker when empty. AC: FR32 snapshot-tested. test_tier: yolo.

title: S3.2 CLI: meshd publish / unpublish (HTTP clients)
description: meshd publish <name> --port N [--txt k=v] and meshd unpublish <name> as thin clients of the control API (FR26/FR27); errors rendered exactly: reserved -> 'reserved and unclaimable' (FR16), conflict -> winner node-id (FR34); nonzero exit codes on failure. test_tier: yolo.

title: S3.1 meshd control API: localhost HTTP/JSON on 127.0.0.1:5380
description: D-002: minimal HTTP/JSON API embedded in mjolnir-meshd, bound 127.0.0.1:5380 (never br-mesh/LAN). POST /v0/publish {name,port,txt?} -> 200 entry | structured error (reserved | owned_by_other{winner_node_id}); POST /v0/unpublish {name}; GET /v0/directory (names, owners, conflicts — the projection bc7's front desk will consume, converging away its file seams). Implementation: tiny_http or hand-rolled over tokio, under the NFR1 size budget. ACs: FR28; reserved/lost publishes return structured errors synchronously. test_tier: smoke.

title: S2.4 Conflict application + loser state
description: On merge Conflict where self is the loser: drop the name from the answer set immediately without waiting for another gossip round (FR33); record lost-name {winner_node_id} for status/API (FR32); mark conflict-lost so publish re-attempts fail synchronously naming the winner (FR34). ACs: FR32-FR34 integration-tested across two in-memory nodes. test_tier: smoke.

title: S2.3 Persistence + boot restore + announce discipline (services.state)
description: services.state sibling of claims.state/addrbook.state, tmp+rename, written on anti-entropy tick + every publish/unpublish (FR22). Boot restore with own-vs-learned split (D-006: owner==self entries are re-owned and re-announced; learned entries served, never re-announced) (FR23). Re-announce own entries each anti-entropy tick like announce_addr_book (FR24). IMMEDIATE gossip broadcast on publish/unpublish, not deferred to tick (FR25 — demo responsiveness). ACs: restart -> own services answerable immediately + re-announced; learned never re-announced. test_tier: smoke.

title: S2.2 Gossip: ServicePublishV2 + ServiceUnpublishV2 appended variants, tombstones (FLAGGED)
description: Two NEW variants appended to GossipMessage (D-004; NEVER mutate legacy ServiceUpdate — postcard would decode-ERROR on old nodes, not skip). Dispatch arms merge into the service store. Tombstone {name, owner_node_id, hlc}: retained in state, HLC-ordered so late re-announces from stale peers lose; same owner re-publishing with newer HLC revives the name (FR31).
design: WHY FLAGGED: wire-format compatibility is the one unrecoverable failure class in a mixed fleet. TEST PLAN (thorough): pinned OLD-schema decoder test (a copy of the pre-e21 GossipMessage enum) fed new-variant bytes -> must decode-skip without error (FR35); new decoder round-trips both variants; tombstone-vs-publish HLC ordering matrix incl. revive; mixed-version integration test. ACs: FR21, FR30, FR31, FR35. PRECEDENT: PeerAddrUpdate (0yb) proved the appended-variant pattern; SubnetClaimRelease is the tombstone shape precedent.

title: S2.1 ServiceEntry v2 + owner-bound merge_service (FLAGGED)
description: ServiceEntry v2: owner_node_id (iroh key), published_at HLC, ip, port, protocol, txt BTreeMap, host_mac Option. merge_service in merge.rs: same-owner newer-HLC=Updated; same-owner older/equal=Unchanged; different-owner=Conflict resolved FWW on FIRST-CLAIM HLC (original owner keeps the name — owner-bound TOFU model), deterministic node-id tiebreak; reserved well-known names rejected at merge.
design: WHY FLAGGED: core correctness — every node must compute the identical winner regardless of gossip order (NFR7). TEST PLAN (thorough): property tests for argument-order symmetry and determinism; two-entry matrices (same/different owner x older/newer/equal HLC x equal-HLC tiebreak); reserved-name rejection; postcard round-trip (FR17). SUBTLETY: 'first-claim HLC' means conflict arbitration compares the ORIGINAL claim HLCs, not latest-update HLCs — an owner refreshing an entry must not weaken or strengthen its claim; carry the first-claim HLC explicitly in the entry if updates re-stamp published_at. ACs: FR17-FR20.

title: S1.4 dnsmasq/UCI wiring: server=/mesh/ line, option 114, DoH canary (FLAGGED)
description: Extend the UCI reconcile: dhcp.@dnsmasq[0].server='/mesh/127.0.0.1#5335'; DHCP option 114 = http://hello.mesh (RFC 8910, bc7 seam contract); server='/use-application-dns.net/' (Firefox DoH canary NXDOMAIN). Idempotent (no restart when config current, same lan_uci_is_current discipline); UCI + init.d restart ONLY (FR13/NFR6 — never edit dnsmasq files, never SIGHUP).
design: RISK (why flagged): this touches fleet-wide client DNS — a malformed server line or failed dnsmasq restart breaks ALL name resolution for clients, not just .mesh. MUST ride mjolnir-apply's health-gated rollback; never apply via live SSH mutation. ORDERING: responder must be bound and answering on 5335 BEFORE uci commit of the forward line (FR14) — sequence in daemon startup, not in the reconcile. TEST PLAN (thorough): idempotence unit tests on the desired-state check; on-device validation: re-run reconcile twice -> zero restarts; kill responder -> dnsmasq SERVFAILs .mesh but upstream resolution unaffected; verify option 114 appears in DHCP offers (tcpdump). ACs: FR9-FR14. EDGE: dnsmasq may retry over TCP for truncated answers — keep responses <=512B (S1.1) so this path never triggers.

title: S1.3 Service answers as pure CRDT projection (A/SRV/TXT)
description: A/SRV/TXT for published services straight from the in-memory service store, no cache (FR8); SRV/TXT from port/protocol/txt map (FR6); TTL 30s. ACs: store mutation visible on the immediately following query; FR3/FR6 unit-tested. test_tier: smoke. Depends on S2.1 (ServiceEntry v2 store).

title: S1.2 Well-known names: hello.mesh/id.mesh answer own gateway IP (pre-claim 192.168.1.1)
description: Compiled-in reserved list (hello, id). A answers = own client gateway IP, or 192.168.1.1 before the /24 is claimed (D-003); TTL 30s; NODATA (NOERROR-empty, never NXDOMAIN) for non-A on existing names. Reserved-name rejection helper exposed for merge (E2) and publish (E3) paths. ACs: FR2, FR5, FR15 unit-tested; FR16 hook in place. test_tier: smoke.

title: S1.1 Responder skeleton: UDP 127.0.0.1:5335 via simple-dns, dispatch + NXDOMAIN default
description: Add simple-dns (daemon feature, D-001), bind UDP 127.0.0.1:5335, parse queries, dispatch by name class, default NXDOMAIN with SOA authority (D-005: hello.mesh. ops.hello.mesh. 1 3600 600 86400 30). Tolerate/ignore EDNS0 OPT; responses <=512B. ACs: malformed packet never panics; unknown .mesh name -> NXDOMAIN+SOA; responder start is sequenced BEFORE the UCI reconcile (FR14 ordering hook). test_tier: smoke. FRs: FR1, FR4, FR7.

title: S8: E2E cross-mesh demo validation (two-node fleet, acceptance)
description: The demo acceptance story. On a two-node 802.11s fleet, fully offline: (1) an identity created at node A's hello.mesh appears in node B's directory within 15s; (2) a service published at A is found in B's directory; (3) page load <2s on the target router; (4) no internet/DNS-upstream required. AC: all four observed end-to-end; captured as the demo runbook. [test_tier: thorough] Depends on the full stack (S1,p6u,S2,avs,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7).

title: S2: service-record gossip — replicate ServiceEntry mesh-wide
description: Wire the existing ServiceEntry type into gossip so a service published at any node converges to all nodes (the focused e21 slice the directory needs — NOT full e21 conflict-resolution/ACLs). AC: a service published at node A appears in node B's converged state within 15s over 802.11s; keyed by service name; expires with its host lease; postcard round-trips. [test_tier: thorough] Depends on the same transport proven by 2xd.
notes: OVERLAP FLAG 2026-07-03 (sprint-002 planning): this duplicates the e21.2 story set (e21.2.1 ServiceEntry v2 + owner-bound merge, e21.2.2 gossip variants, e21.2.3 persistence/announce) planned from docs/prd-mesh-naming-first-stone.md with ADRs in docs/sprints/002-mesh-naming/architecture-decisions.md. Recommend closing this as duplicate and letting bc7 depend on e21.2.3 instead — Duke to confirm.

title: S1: /users CRDT record type + gossip sync (productionize the spike)
description: MOSTLY DONE by spike 2xd (commit ed011d2): the /users CRDT record type, GossipMessage::UserUpdate, merge_user() LWW, and the e2e convergence/LWW/stale-discard tests all landed in the lib (Mac-testable, no daemon feature). REMAINING (this story) = daemon WIRING only: apply an incoming UserUpdate into a live UserBook in the meshd run loop + anti-entropy re-broadcast, mirroring the PeerAddrUpdate/subnet-claim handlers; persist like addr_book. AC: meshd applies a received UserUpdate into its UserBook and re-broadcasts; a user written on node A is observable in node B's daemon state; persisted across restart. NOTE: this is meshd-binary code = builds/tests on Linux only (see hg0); the record/merge logic is already proven in the lib. [test_tier: thorough] Unblocked (2xd done).

title: /services/ CRDT lane: ServiceEntry v2, merge_service, gossip apply, publish surface (wiki.mesh)
description: Second half of the e21 first stone (docs/network-coordination/mesh-naming.md). ServiceEntry v2: add owner_node_id + published_at HLC, host_mac becomes Option (node-hosted services first; device-published waits for the lease lane). New merge_service in merge.rs: same-owner newer-HLC = Updated (anti-entropy re-announce like the 0yb addr book); different owner = Conflict, FWW on HLC, loser logs loud and stops answering. Reserved well-known names rejected at merge. Daemon: apply ServiceUpdate in dispatch, persist (services.state sibling of claims/addrbook), re-announce on anti-entropy tick, serve via the .mesh responder (A + SRV + TXT). Publish surface on the same control path as status: meshd publish <name> --port N / unpublish (tombstone). Mixed-fleet safe (old nodes decode-skip). Expiry deferred to 99f (one staleness design for all self-announced lanes).
notes: PRD validated 2026-07-03: docs/prd-mesh-naming-first-stone.md (status: validated, 0 blockers; covers e21.1+e21.2 as MVP FR1-FR35). Key deltas vs original bead description: (1) conflict model upgraded to OWNER-BOUND names (TOFU: first claim binds name to owner_node_id, FWW on FIRST-CLAIM HLC so the original owner keeps the name; provenance shown in status + directory); (2) mixed-fleet: do NOT evolve the existing ServiceUpdate variant schema — postcard decode would ERROR on old nodes; add a NEW appended ServicePublishV2 variant; (3) publish/unpublish gossip broadcast is IMMEDIATE, not deferred to the anti-entropy tick (demo responsiveness); (4) publish needs a write-path IPC to the running daemon (file-spool vs unix socket = open question; status is read-only today); (5) service A-record IP = publisher's client gateway IP; (6) open questions listed in PRD: tombstone wire format, SOA field values, hello.mesh answer before /24 claim, own-vs-learned service restore split in services.state. WoT arbitration needs captured in e21.5.

title: .mesh DNS responder: meshd serves the .mesh zone behind dnsmasq (hello.mesh + well-known names)
description: First stone of the e21 naming design (docs/network-coordination/mesh-naming.md). Embed an authoritative DNS responder for .mesh in mjolnir-meshd on 127.0.0.1:5335 (NOT 5353 — mDNS), pure projection of the in-memory CRDT. Wire dnsmasq via UCI only: dhcp.@dnsmasq[0].server='/mesh/127.0.0.1#5335' plus the DoH canary server='/use-application-dns.net/' (same reconcile discipline as reconcile_client_uci — never edit dnsmasq files, never SIGHUP). Answers: A with TTL 30s; NODATA (NOERROR-empty) for non-A on existing names, never NXDOMAIN on AAAA; NXDOMAIN for unknown names. Ships the compiled-in reserved well-known list (hello.mesh, id.mesh) answered anycast-by-convention with this node's own client gateway IP — never in the CRDT, unclaimable. Unblocks the rp9 front desk origin.
notes: Also owns the hello-mesh-service.md §5 seam contract: the UCI reconcile sets DHCP option 114 (RFC 8910, non-blocking affordance per rp9) to http://hello.mesh alongside the server=/mesh/ forward line. Related epic: bc7 (front desk build) — it stops at this seam; e21.1 makes hello.mesh actually resolve.

title: Decide: IPv6 overlay vs IPv4 subnet claims (addressing is a limited resource)
description: IPv4 may not reach the destination: nodes claiming whole /24s out of 10.42.0.0/16 hands out a limited resource (255 nodes max per mesh, waste per claim), and squatting/collisions get worse at scale. IPv6 (ULA per-mesh prefix, or GUA) removes the scarcity entirely, gives every node/service a stable address, and improves interop/compat with existing mesh ecosystems (LibreMesh et al). Known costs: much harder UX, debugging, and support; dual-stack complexity during transition; babeld handles IPv6 natively (point in favor). Deliverable: a decision doc — evaluate IPv6-first overlay, dual-stack, and IPv4-with-NAT-tricks against the service-mesh phase requirements. Blocks the service-mesh architecture pass epic.
notes: DECISION DOC DRAFTED 2026-07-02: docs/network-coordination/ipv6-addressing-decision.md, status PROPOSED. Recommends Option C — IPv6 spine / IPv4 edge: ULA /48 per mesh (RFC 4193, derived from mesh identity), 64-bit blake3(node_id) /128 node addresses (kills the ~300-node 10.254/16 birthday-collision cliff), v4-via-v6 backhaul next-hops (RFC 9229; shipped babeld 1.13 supports it, fe80 already on br-mesh), client /24s unchanged, /subnets6 claim lane reusing the existing CRDT for client /64s. NAT rejected on ethos; v6-only rejected on client reality. 4 shippable migration phases, P1-P2 client-invisible. AWAITING RATIFICATION by Duke — ratify to close and unblock e21.
close_reason: DECIDED: stay IPv4; IPv6 rejected as not worth the risk. Decision doc: docs/network-coordination/ipv6-addressing-decision.md. Decisive insight: per-service addressing (the requirement that forced v6) is already handled by iroh node-ids — location-independent, no scarcity. Adversarial review killed the v6-spine draft: RFC 6724 ULA precedence means v4 wins on dual-published names anyway; v4-via-v6 unproven against mt7986 mtk_ppe offload; PMTU black-hole risk over mjolnir0; permanent dual-stack ops tax. Collision cliff fixed natively instead: mjolnir-mesh-pt9 (backhaul claims on the existing CRDT + deterministic re-derivation). Revisit triggers documented: giant single 802.11s island, or hard v6-interop requirement. e21 unblocked.

title: Architecture pass: broadcast peer/service discovery + conflict resolution (service mesh phase)
description: North Star check + design pass now that the data plane is field-validated. The founding vision is the destination: same SSID across nodes, devices roam, services broadcast on the local mesh and discoverable via .mesh — accessible locally AND over the internet via the iroh L3 overlay. Ethos constraints are hard requirements: censorship-resistant, truly peer-to-peer, no implicit authority, ad hoc join/leave, scalable. The gossip+CRDT design (docs/network-coordination/gossip-and-crdt.md, archived dhcp-crdt.md) was sound; we detoured to per-node routed /24s + babel-over-802.11s-L2 to get a working mesh. Lesson learned: local mesh routes most efficiently over the L2 island; iroh L3 is for internet hops and as a security gateway on first hop. This pass designs: (1) broadcast peer discovery, (2) service discovery/announcement (the service mesh — local .mesh access + internet access via iroh L3), (3) conflict resolution moving onto our L3 as originally expected. Relates to 0yb (gossip address book — likely the first concrete stone). Depends on the IPv6 decision bead.
design: NAMING DESIGN DECIDED 2026-07-03 (item 2 of this pass): docs/network-coordination/mesh-naming.md. Decisions: (a) resolution = daemon-embedded authoritative DNS responder for .mesh on 127.0.0.1:5335, dnsmasq forwards via one UCI line (server=/mesh/...), pure projection of the in-memory CRDT — no dnsmasq file management; (b) namespace = FLAT name.mesh with compiled-in reserved well-known list (hello.mesh, id.mesh = anycast-by-convention, every node answers with itself; required by rp9 same-origin browser keys); (c) arbitration = FWW on HLC same as subnet claims, squatting accepted until web-of-trust identity binding; (d) local+internet access falls out free: A-answers are mesh IPs, babel routes same-island over L2 and cross-site into mjolnir0 — DNS never branches on locality, iroh node-id in the record is provenance not resolution; (e) first stone = e21.1 (responder + well-known) + e21.2 (/services/ lane v2 + publish surface); device names (e21.3) and mDNS reflection (e21.4) deferred. Sharp edges documented in the doc: DoH canary NXDOMAIN, .mesh not IANA special-use, secure-context handled by rp9 rungs. Still owned by this pass: item 1 broadcast peer discovery north-star check (0yb largely answers it — confirm), item 3 conflict resolution on our L3, and the relay pinning decision (production n0 vs self-hosted).
notes: Folded in mjolnir-mesh-3hs (relay pinning): canary is already gone — default is RelayMode::Staging (real n0 relays) with a repeatable --relay override. Remaining decision, now owned by this architecture pass: for field-deployed nodes doing cross-site iroh, pin production n0 relays vs self-hosted relays (sovereignty/censorship-resistance — the ethos constraint). Relays only matter for the internet-hop / L3-overlay side of the service mesh; local mesh stays --lan/relay-free. Decide alongside item (2) service discovery over iroh L3.

title: claim lifecycle: node re-claims a fresh /24 on restart (treats own restored claim as foreign) and stale claims/addrs are never retracted
description: Two live manifestations 2026-07-01: (1) m3000-b restart: 'restored subnet claims from disk count=1' (its own 10.42.12.0/24) then 8s later 'claimed client subnet and published it subnet=10.42.13.0/24' — it bumped off its OWN restored claim and now holds/broadcasts BOTH (anti-entropy count=2), with both .1/24 addresses on br-lan. (2) m3000 (10.254.242.172) and wr3000s-a (10.254.242.84) BOTH have 10.42.242.1/24 on br-lan (blake3-first-byte collision, same preferred slot as their shared backhaul high byte 242); CRDT winner is m3000 (fleet routes point there) but the loser wr3000s-a never re-claimed elsewhere and never retracted its local address, leaving it with no routable client subnet. Fix area: claim_manager/alloc self-identification on restore, collision-loser reassignment, and retracting local addr when a claim is lost/superseded. Related: nrh (re-publish), 99f (stale-claim lifecycle).
notes: Convergence half implemented in a415b30 (on top of the restart/retract half in 512c4e8): root cause of the fleet-wide non-convergence found live on the gateway node — iroh-gossip bootstrap join is a one-shot dial that fires before the radio/mDNS are up ('dial failed: No addressing information available' 10s after boot, then never retried; anti-entropy count pinned at 1 forever). Fix: gossip_rejoin_loop re-issues join_peers whenever neighbor count is 0 (capped backoff 5-60s, mirrors connector_loop); NeighborUp/Down now logged + tracked via watch channel; fresh nodes gate their first claim on join + one full anti-entropy period (25s) instead of the blind 8s warmup that re-collided by construction; restored-claim nodes claim immediately. VALIDATION PLAN: deploy new build to all 4 nodes, restart wr3000s-a (currently stopped on purpose) LAST, watch for 'gossip: neighbor up' on all nodes, anti-entropy count reaching 4-ish fleet-wide, wr3000s-a losing 242 to m3000 via first-writer-wins, retracting 10.42.242.1 and re-claiming a different /24.
close_reason: Field-validated 2026-07-02 on the 4-node fleet, build 65a7782. Full sequence observed live on wr3000s-a: restored claim re-published as-is (no fresh /24 on restart), gossip joined instantly via seeded derived address (10.254.x:49737, no discovery), m3000's senior 242 claim won first-writer-wins, wr3000s-a retracted 10.42.242.1 from br-lan and re-claimed 10.42.243.0/24 — all within 6s of start. Anti-entropy count=4 on all four nodes (first full CRDT convergence ever). Three fixes: 512c4e8 (reuse own claim / release duplicates / retract on loss), a415b30 (gossip rejoin loop + join-gated claiming), 65a7782 (derived-address seeding — root cause of the island: dial-by-id had no addressing info, mDNS unreliable over 802.11s).

title: client DHCP still serves stock 192.168.1.0/24 on every node — claimed /24 must own br-lan/dnsmasq
description: Found live 2026-07-01: meshd's assign_client_addr only ALIASES the claimed 10.42.x.1/24 onto br-lan; the stock 192.168.1.1/24 stays primary and dnsmasq's dhcp.lan pool serves from it. Every node hands clients 192.168.1.x from an identical, fleet-ambiguous subnet -> cross-mesh replies exit the far node's own br-lan and black-hole (laptop on tr3000 could ping local 10.42.61.1 but nothing across the mesh; mgmt 10.254.x also unreachable from clients). Fix direction: claimed /24 becomes the lan primary (uci network.lan.ipaddr + netmask, dnsmasq follows) reconciled by meshd or the apply pipeline on claim change; decide fate of the 192.168.1.1 wired-recovery convention (keep as alias?). Interim bench fix is a manual uci renumber per node.
close_reason: Field-validated 2026-07-02, build 93e1459 on all 4 nodes. reconcile_client_uci (claim manager, every claim apply): claimed gw CIDR becomes primary network.lan.ipaddr, 192.168.1.1/24 kept as wired-recovery alias, dnsmasq restarted. Verified per node: uci primary = claimed /24, dhcp-range regenerated in claimed subnet (wr3000s-a serves 10.42.243.x — composes with the eon conflict re-claim), and cross-mesh client-path pings claimed-gw→claimed-gw succeed from tr3000 to all three peers (the field black-hole case). Handles claim changes (conflict loser re-claims → primary rewritten). Note: stale network.client/br-client orphan sections (pre-e4r container-gateway era) still exist on some nodes — harmless, cleanup candidate.

title: babeld redistribute 'ge N le N' filter never matches — claimed /24 not announced into babel
description: Found live on the fleet 2026-07-01 during Tier-1 client validation. Rendered filter 'redistribute ip 10.42.12.0/24 ge 24 le 24 allow' (babel/config.rs render_babeld_conf, and check render_overlay too) exports NOTHING: babeld's SIGUSR1 dump shows only local /32 addresses exported, never the claimed /24, so far-node client subnets are unroutable (only .1/32 leaks via implicit local export). Empirically proven on m3000-b: inserting bare 'redistribute ip 10.42.12.0/24 allow' above the deny + babeld bounce -> /24 exported immediately and installed on tr3000 as '10.42.12.0/24 via 10.254.12.214'. Fix: drop the ge/le qualifiers (or use babeld's 'eq'). NOTE: m3000-b still carries the hand-inserted sed line in /etc/mjolnir/babeld.conf as a live workaround; it reverts on next meshd conf render.
close_reason: Fixed in a657baa and deployed fleet-wide via update-fleet.sh (health-gated, all 4 nodes OK). Bare 'redistribute ip <subnet> allow' renders in both LAN and overlay configs; verified live: all claimed /24s propagate and install fleet-wide, conf now survives meshd restarts (code-rendered, no more sed erosion). Same commit: br-mesh renders 'type wireless enable-timestamps rtt-min 10 rtt-max 120 max-rtt-penalty 150' and 'redistribute local deny' (junk /32 exports gone, verified in route tables).

title: meshd: direct iroh tunnel drops ~36s in (MultipathNotNegotiated) and does not recover
description: HARDWARE FINDING (apo/4pk, .181/.113, 2026-06-24). The 4pk DIRECT path forms (kind=DIRECT 10.254.x rtt~13ms) and works for ~36s, then the iroh connection/path closes with 'noq_proto::connection: failed closing path err=MultipathNotNegotiated' (also seen earlier as 'tunnel closed reason=TimedOut'). serve_tunnel drops the per-peer TUN on conn.closed() -> the mj-peer interface disappears -> babeld config goes empty -> babeld 'Eek no interfaces'. The connector_loop redials but the tunnel does not stay up (death loop persists minutes). So the direct path is not STABLE. MultipathNotNegotiated is an iroh 1.0 multipath/path-migration error — likely iroh tries to migrate/close the relay path after the direct path forms and multipath isn't negotiated, dropping the connection. INVESTIGATE: iroh 1.0 multipath config / path migration behavior; whether to enable multipath, pin the direct path, or hold the connection across path changes; idle-timeout/keepalive on the QUIC connection; whether the LAN-only (no relay) endpoint mishandles the single-path case. This is the real blocker for stable cross-mesh routing now that the backhaul/direct-path forms. Lower-layer than babeld (2zz).
notes: HANDOFF DOC written: docs/network-coordination/auu-routing-handoff.md (topology map, the duplicate-IP nexthop blocker, 4 fix options, full routing-method history). TOPOLOGY CONFIRMED on hardware: both RouterOS hosts have 172.20.0.1/24 on br-mesh and both containers 172.20.0.2 — DUPLICATED across the shared L2 broadcast domain. Only 10.254.x/16 is unique/segment-routable. babel installs nexthop=172.20.0.2 (placeholder, wrong) -> no forwarding. Recommended fix: Option B (separate private host<->container link from the shared-L2 backhaul iface so babel uses 10.254.x). User taking over in a new agent window.
close_reason: Obsolete: per-peer iroh TUN tunnels are retired/off-by-default (--lan-tunnels only remains as a retest flag); cross-site traffic goes over the single overlay TUN mjolnir0 (buw), local mesh over 802.11s L2. If MultipathNotNegotiated instability reappears on mjolnir0's iroh path, file a fresh bead against that architecture.

title: meshd: babeld dies on SIGHUP reload and is never restarted
description: HARDWARE FINDING (apo/4pk, routers .181/.113): with the 4pk DIRECT backhaul path up, the per-peer TUN tunnel forms kind=DIRECT rtt~13ms, but cross-mesh routing still fails because the ACCEPTOR's babeld dies. Sequence on .113: 'spawned babeld pid=6' -> 'babeld started' -> ~5s later 'babeld config changed — reloaded via SIGHUP' -> babeld goes SILENT forever (with -d 1 it logs constantly, so it exited). .181's babeld stays alive but its neighbour 'reach' decays to ~0 (rxcost 65535) because it stops hearing .113's hellos. The reconciler uses bare BabelSupervisor::spawn (NOT babel::run_with_restart), so a dead babeld is never revived. LEAD: an earlier run logged 'creat(/var/run/babeld.pid): File exists' — babeld's pidfile (-I default /var/run/babeld.pid) likely conflicts across reload/respawn. FIX DIRECTIONS: (1) wire babel::run_with_restart so babeld is respawned on death (it exists, unused); (2) stop babeld dying on SIGHUP — give it a clean/unique pidfile (-I) or clear it before (re)start, or verify SIGHUP-reload works in babeld 1.13 (maybe restart instead of SIGHUP); (3) reduce SIGHUP churn (only on meaningful config change). This is the last blocker for cross-mesh routing now that the direct path works.
notes: ROOT CAUSE (2026-06-24): babeld dies because (a) it exits on SIGHUP reload, and (b) it refuses to run with zero interfaces ('Eek... asked to run on no interfaces!'). The acceptor's babeld 'death' was actually the per-peer TUN disappearing when the direct tunnel dropped (see the new tunnel-stability bead mjolnir-mesh-auu) -> empty config -> Eek. FIX COMMITTED (de1f20a): supervisor clears stale pidfile + has restart()/has_exited(); reconciler restarts babeld on config-change (not SIGHUP), respawns on exit, and keeps babeld STOPPED while zero tunnels are live (no more Eek-loop). NOT YET redeployed/validated on hardware because the tunnel-stability bug (mjolnir-mesh-auu) keeps the interface set empty — fix that first, then this should give a stable adjacency.
close_reason: Fixed and hardened: babeld is restarted (not SIGHUP'd) on config change — babeld 1.13 exits on SIGHUP, and the shipped babel/config.rs codifies restart-on-change; stale pidfile clearing + respawn-on-exit committed (de1f20a); procd supervision + exact-cmdline stray-babeld reap (nrr) validated fleet-wide. The blocking auu context (per-peer TUN churn) is retired.

title: Build the overlay for OpenWrt mt76 nodes (aarch64-musl, native, + babeld pkg)
description: New mesh nodes are OpenWrt mt76 boards (MT7981/MT7986, aarch64 Cortex-A53) running the iroh+babeld overlay NATIVELY (not in a RouterOS container). Today we only cross-build armv7-musl for the MikroTik container (deploy/mikrotik). Add an aarch64-unknown-linux-musl build of mjolnir-meshd, wire up babeld (in OpenWrt's repo), and a small install/run recipe for OpenWrt (no container needed -- static musl binary runs directly). This is what makes 'runs on OpenWrt nodes' real and is the precursor to standing up the 802.11s island (b1d).
close_reason: aarch64-musl build script + native install recipe done; static binary validated on arm64 Linux (LAN mode, 10.254 backhaul self-assigned + surfaced by iroh). babeld via opkg.

title: Multi-hop discovery: babeld/mDNS synchronization (gossip-based address book)
description: Everything validated so far assumes ONE shared L2 segment, where mDNS floods to all nodes so everyone discovers everyone directly. A spread-out forest has no such segment: flat mDNS only reaches direct radio-neighbors. babeld routes TRAFFIC multi-hop fine, but a node learning how to ADDRESS a non-neighbor (the address iroh needs to dial) is unsolved by mDNS alone. Direction: stop relying on flat mDNS for mesh-wide discovery; use the roster + propagate peer addresses (node id -> reachable address) over the CRDT gossip overlay (which rides the babeld-routed IP network), the same way subnet claims propagate. mDNS stays for direct-neighbor bootstrap; gossip becomes an eventually-consistent mesh-wide address book. This synchronization between link-local discovery (mDNS) and the routed overlay (babeld+gossip) is the protocol to design+build. Platform-independent. See docs/network-coordination/radio-backhaul-and-discovery.md.
notes: COMPLETE 2026-07-03: m3000-b returned, peer list flattened, updated to 53a26ec (health gate passed, 4 babel neighbors). Four-way addrbook convergence validated: every node learned all 3 other peers' entries; validate-fleet.sh VALIDATION OK across 4 nodes, zero collisions.
close_reason: Deployed fleet-wide (build 53a26ec) and field-validated 2026-07-03: all 3 live nodes (wr3000s-a, m3000, tr3000; m3000-b offline/skipped) converged — every node learned both other peers' PeerAddrUpdate entries over gossip ('addrbook: learned peer address' in logread), addrbook.state persisted, status prints the book, iroh lookup fed. Field finding during validation: fleet peer lists were a fragile CHAIN (each node exactly one bootstrap peer; wr3000s-a's only peer was offline m3000-b → gossip island, zero CRDT traffic). Fixed by flattening to full peer sets in /etc/config/mjolnir on all 3 live nodes. TTL/expiry deferred to 99f; docs surfacing deferred to ysw.

title: Validate 4-node mesh on the wired switch (real babeld + client data path)
description: Bring up all 4 nodes on the shared wired switch with the new LAN-mode/IPv4-backhaul build. Apply container-net-lan.rsc ($meshLink = switch-facing port) on each, deploy the fresh image, run 'mesh' (defaults --lan) with a roster of all 4 ids, REAL babeld (not stubbed). Validate: (a) each node assigns its 10.254 backhaul; (b) all 6 pairs form kind=DIRECT tunnels (path logger); (c) CRDT converges, 4 distinct /24 claims; (d) babeld propagates all /24s; (e) a client on node A's LAN reaches a host on node B's LAN across the mesh. This is the hardware proof of the 4pk mechanism before the wireless backhaul swap. Blocks the wireless-backhaul work (prove software on the wire first).
close_reason: 2j6: every acceptance item (backhaul assignment, DIRECT tunnels, CRDT convergence w/ 4 distinct /24s, babeld propagation, cross-LAN client traffic) field-validated over 802.11s radio backhaul — strictly harder than the wired switch this specified; purpose (prove software before wireless swap) overtaken. b1d: 802.11s island shipped as the fleet; AP/STA segment + interop gateway retired by decision 2026-07-02 (AP/STA strictly worse than 802.11s).

title: LAN-mode backhaul: container on shared L2 + IPv6 link-local node discovery
description: Pivot the same-site mesh off the relay/double-NAT path. (1) Put the meshd container on the shared L2 segment (bridge veth-mesh to the switch-facing port) so peers are mutually reachable instead of buried behind each node's masquerade. (2) Default the daemon to --lan (mDNS + relays disabled, fully offline). (3) Node-to-node backhaul addressing via IPv6 link-local (zero-config, collision-free, already proven for babeld in op4), with a node-id-derived IPv4 fallback if iroh mDNS can't dial link-local. Unifies the address space so all 4 same-room nodes form DIRECT iroh paths; sidesteps mjolnir-mesh-67h (no relay exists offline). CRDT keeps overlay/client /24 addressing unchanged.
notes: DIRECT PATH HARDWARE-VALIDATED 2026-06-24 (routers .181/.113). Containers bridged onto the shared mgmt LAN (no dedicated backhaul port on this bench, so veth-mesh added to the default bridge — additive, mgmt/SSH untouched). meshd self-assigned 10.254.x (.181=10.254.23.7, .113=10.254.3.43) and the per-peer tunnel formed kind=DIRECT remote=10.254.x rtt~13ms, NO RELAY. KEY FIX (committed c592ecc): the RouterOS container names the NIC 'veth-mesh', NOT eth0 — backhaul iface discovery is now name-agnostic via /sys/class/net (sole non-loopback fallback + startup-race retry). REMAINING 4pk hardware items: babeld routing of client /24s is blocked by the new babeld-dies-on-SIGHUP bug (see mjolnir-mesh-2zz); 4-node (not 2) on the switch still to do.
close_reason: Superseded: RouterOS shared-L2 container backhaul retired for 802.11s (see decision-2026-07-02-ap-sta-radio-architecture; data plane field-validated on the 4-node OpenWrt fleet). Its learnings (LAN-default, derived 10.254/16 addressing) are absorbed in the shipped design.

title: meshd: TUN needs an IPv6 link-local for babeld to form an adjacency
description: HARDWARE FINDING (apo, routers .181/.113): babeld parses its config and runs, but installs NO cross-mesh route — the per-peer mj-peer-X TUN is created with only an IPv4 /31, and Babel runs its protocol over IPv6 link-local (fe80::), exchanging both v4 and v6 routes over it. With no IPv6 LL on the TUN, babeld can't send/receive Hellos -> no neighbour -> no routes. Symptom: ping A->B (10.42.23.1 -> 10.42.3.1) returns 'TTL exceeded' from the container (172.20.0.2) — the packet reaches container A but has no tunnel route for B's /24, so it loops container<->router until TTL dies. (This also proves the RouterOS->container->forward data path is CORRECT; only babeld's route is missing.) FIX: in tun/iface.rs spawn_tunnel, ensure the TUN has an IPv6 link-local — either enable IPv6 on the iface (net.ipv6.conf.<if>.disable_ipv6=0 + addr_gen_mode) or explicitly assign a deterministic fe80:: address per side via rtnetlink (like the /31 v4 assign). Also bump babeld verbosity (-d 1/2) in the supervisor so neighbour/route activity is visible (babeld is silent at default level).
close_reason: FIXED + hardware-validated. IPv6 link-local on the TUN (fe80::<host-octet>) lets babeld form a Babel adjacency: .181 logged 'Neighbour fe80::d5 dev mj-peer-9b8c14e' and installed '10.42.3.0/24 ... via mj-peer ... (installed)'. Also dropped babeld -D (was daemonising/detaching it) and added -d 1 for visibility. babeld now routes; the residual ping failure is asymmetric data-plane delivery (67h), not op4.

title: meshd: install local /24 back-route on claim (so babeld can redistribute it)
description: HARDWARE FINDING (apo Tier-2): babeld starts with ifaces=1 but has nothing to redistribute — the container has no route for its OWN claimed /24, so 'redistribute ip 10.42.x.0/24' matches nothing and no cross-mesh route propagates. This is the 'per-router /24 -> local LAN' caveat from client-routing.md. Fix: when meshd claims /24 X, also install a kernel route 'X via <container-gateway 172.20.0.1> dev eth0' in the container netns (rtnetlink, like the TUN setup). That (a) gives babeld a concrete route to redistribute and (b) sends inbound mesh traffic for X back to the router for local-client delivery. Needs the container gateway (derive from default route, or a --client-gateway flag).
close_reason: FIXED + hardware-validated. meshd installs '<claimed /24> via 172.20.0.1' on claim (--client-gateway, default 172.20.0.1). Both routers logged 'installed client subnet route subnet=10.42.x.0/24 gateway=172.20.0.1'; babeld then redistributes the /24 (peer's babeld learned + installed it). Confirmed on hardware.

title: meshd: babeld stdio should inherit, not pipe (deadlock + visibility)
description: HARDWARE FINDING (apo Tier-2, .181/.113): BabelSupervisor::spawn sets .stdout(Stdio::piped()).stderr(Stdio::piped()) but nothing drains them. babeld -D logs to stdout; once the ~64KB pipe buffer fills, babeld BLOCKS on write and hangs (routing silently dies). Also we get zero babeld visibility — can't see neighbor/route exchange in the container log. Fix: use Stdio::inherit() so babeld's output flows to the container log (RouterOS captures it), or actively drain the pipes. Inherit also gives us the route-exchange diagnostics we currently lack.
close_reason: FIXED + hardware-validated. babeld stdio now inherited (was piped+undrained); babeld's own logs reach the container log — which is how the config parse error AND the txcost/asymmetry were diagnosed. Also dropped -D so babeld stays a foreground child (valid PID, working SIGHUP).

title: meshd: multi-peer tunnel manager (N concurrent /31 tunnels)
description: Today the daemon runs exactly one tunnel (one tun-listen OR one tun-connect). Replace with a manager that maintains a peer->Tunnel map: dial/accept every peer in the roster, bring up a per-peer /31 TUN via spawn_tunnel, redial on drop, tear down on removal, and expose the live set of peer-interface names (needed by babeld). Foundation for routing — babel and forwarding both key off the live tunnel set.
notes: IMPLEMENTED + smoke-tested (off-hardware). New 'mesh --roster <file>' subcommand runs the full multi-peer daemon: builds endpoint, loads PeerRoster (kui), accepts inbound TUN_ALPN tunnels AND dials peers. Design: (1) ONE tunnel per peer-pair via node-id tie-break — the lexicographically-lower id dials, the higher accepts (mirrors pick_link_31's ordering) so both ends never race to create the same deterministic /31 'mj-peer-<id>' interface. (2) Shared TunnelRegistry (Arc<Mutex<HashMap<EndpointId,String>>>) maps peer->iface, enforces one-tunnel-per-peer atomically (sentinel reserve), and is the seam babeld (83k) will read for the live tunnel set. (3) serve_tunnel() shared by accept + dial paths. (4) connector_loop per dial-peer: dial -> serve -> redial with capped (1s..30s) exponential backoff, resets on success. Local smoke test (self ac9c.. < two peer ids) confirmed: roster loaded, both dialed, 'mesh up dialing=2', redial fired after dial-fail. 136 tests pass, clippy clean. NOT YET hardware-validated: real TUN-per-peer bring-up + concurrent multi-peer is Linux+root — rides on bead apo (e2e). FOLLOW-UPS (noted, not blocking): roster is loaded once (no dynamic SIGHUP reload yet); registry not yet exposed via a public getter (83k adds that); bare-id roster entries need relay/discovery to resolve addrs (use full address blobs in relay-less mode).
close_reason: Multi-peer tunnel manager implemented + locally smoke-tested (roster load, id tie-break, per-peer redial/backoff, one-tunnel-per-peer registry). Builds on hardware-validated spawn_tunnel. Concurrent multi-peer hardware e2e tracked in mjolnir-mesh-apo.

title: meshd: peer roster / membership source (which peers to dial)
description: The daemon currently learns a single peer from a pasted address blob (tun-connect <addr>). For a real mesh it needs a set of peers to maintain connections to. MVP: a static roster file (node addresses) the daemon reads and dials/accepts; later replaced/augmented by CRDT membership. Output: a resolved, updatable peer set consumed by the multi-peer tunnel manager.
notes: LIB INCREMENT LANDED (ultrawork): src/roster.rs — PeerRoster/PeerEntry/RosterError, load()/load_from_str(), file format (token + optional label, # comments, blank lines, first-wins dedup, empty-ok). 8 unit tests, lib stays iroh-free (tokens stored verbatim; daemon parses). REMAINING: daemon CLI flag (e.g. --roster <file>) + actually dialing/accepting the peer set — that consumption is part of mjolnir-mesh-23a (multi-peer manager), which depends on this.
close_reason: Peer roster membership source complete: src/roster.rs (PeerRoster, 8 tests) landed AND now consumed by the daemon — 'mesh --roster <file>' (23a) loads it and dials the peer set. Daemon-reads-and-dials requirement satisfied.

title: Data plane: carry client LAN traffic across the mesh (tr6 P2+P3)
description: Today mjolnir-meshd is the P1 connectivity proof: it brings up ONE per-peer /31 TUN (tun-listen/tun-connect) and UDP-probes between the two link addresses. It does NOT yet carry a wifi/LAN client's traffic across the mesh — the binary references none of the babel/alloc/crdt modules, which exist only as libraries. This epic wires the 'top layer' so a client on router A can reach a host on router B's LAN: multi-peer tunnels + subnet claim (alloc/CRDT) + babeld routing + container IP forwarding + RouterOS route glue. Groups tr6's P2 (multi-peer + subnet alloc + CRDT gossip) and P3 (babeld cross-site routing). Done when the e2e client-traffic test passes on hardware.
close_reason: Epic done-condition met: e2e client-traffic test passed on hardware (apo, 2026-07-01 real-laptop field proof + 2026-07-02 re-validation on deployed code with zero workarounds — far-node client reached all claimed gateways, mgmt plane, internet via a8o gateway, DNS; reverse babel routes confirmed). All 7 children closed. The top layer — multi-peer + subnet claim (alloc/CRDT) + babeld routing + forwarding — carries real client LAN traffic across the mesh.

title: meshd tunnel data-plane reliability: only 1/5 probes cross
description: On the first live two-router mesh (meshctl deploy --all, routers .181/.113), the tun-connect tunnel established and IP traffic crossed (DATA PLANE WORKS) but only 1/5 UDP probes got replies — 4 timed out. Investigate: relay-only path (no hole-punch yet?) loss/latency, MTU on the /31 TUN over iroh datagrams, probe timing vs tunnel warmup, or RouterOS container UDP handling. Reproduce via the connector container log. Not a blocker for connectivity but needed for a usable mesh.
notes: ADDRESSED by 4pk's direct path (2026-06-24). Root cause was relay-path loss/asymmetry; with the direct LAN backhaul the per-peer tunnel is kind=DIRECT rtt~13ms (no relay), so the data plane is reliable. Not formally re-measuring the old 1/5 probe yet because the routing test is blocked by the separate babeld-dies-on-SIGHUP bug (mjolnir-mesh-2zz), but the relay-asymmetry that caused 67h is structurally gone. Close once a clean client-traffic test passes on the direct path.
close_reason: Obsolete: the 1/5-probe relay-path loss was in the retired per-peer-tunnel/RouterOS architecture; notes already recorded it structurally gone with the direct path. Data plane since rebuilt on 802.11s and field-validated (tr6/0xb closed).

title: P1.5: --lan mode — mDNS LAN discovery (no relay/internet)
description: Added --lan: Endpoint::empty_builder(RelayMode::Disabled) + MdnsAddressLookup (iroh address-lookup-mdns feature). No pkarr/n0-DNS (no internet dependency, no spam), relays off, mDNS peer discovery on the LAN. tun-connect/connect accept a bare 64-hex node id (parse_peer), resolved via mDNS. Validated locally on macOS: connect-by-node-id → mDNS resolves → connection established, no relay. Unblocks same-switch swarm testing without fighting container internet egress / canary relays. REQUIRES containers on the same L2 LAN (bridged, not NAT'd) for mDNS multicast + direct reachability.
close_reason: Shipped: --lan is now the DEFAULT mesh mode (RelayMode::Disabled + MdnsAddressLookup in mjolnir-meshd.rs), validated on hardware. Bead described work already done at filing time.

title: P1 probe: TUN-in-RouterOS-container (mjolnir-meshd tun-test)
description: Gating check for the L3 data plane on MikroTik: does a TUN device create inside a RouterOS container (needs /dev/net/tun + CAP_NET_ADMIN)? Added 'tun-test' subcommand that creates a throwaway /31 via PeerInterface::create and tears it down. If it works, full P1 (IrohDatagramConn + spawn_encap_pair + connect-and-tunnel) is viable on MikroTik; if not, rethink packet path (userspace, etc.). Test on the 4-router LAN testbed. Also fixed latent breakage: tun/iface.rs never compiled for linux target (missing futures-util dep + tun::Error conversion) — now cross-compiles.
notes: ANSWERED YES (2026-06-23): TUN-in-RouterOS-container WORKS. During meshctl deploy --all on .113, the tun-connect container brought up a TUN device (iface mj-peer-81f543c, /31 10.255.53.213<->.212) and a UDP probe crossed it: 'tunnel ping 1: reply from 10.255.53.212 in 168ms — DATA PLANE WORKS'. So /dev/net/tun + CAP_NET_ADMIN are available in the RouterOS container. Note: only 1/5 probes replied (reliability follow-up filed).
close_reason: TUN-in-container confirmed viable on hardware: tunnel established + IP packet crossed (DATA PLANE WORKS)

title: P0: iroh connectivity MVP (mjolnir-meshd id/listen/connect)
description: First slice of the headless daemon (tr6). Minimal [[bin]] mjolnir-meshd in crates/mjolnir-mesh behind a 'daemon' feature (keeps lib + tests iroh-free). Subcommands: id (print EndpointId + addr blob), listen (accept on mjolnir/mesh/v0 ALPN, echo ping datagrams), connect <addr> (dial, round-trip a datagram, report RTT). Persisted identity via --secret-file (gen on first run) / IROH_SECRET. NO TUN — de-risks iroh-in-container before the TUN-in-container question. Reuses iroh 0.96 patterns from mjolnir-node.
acceptance_criteria: cargo build --features daemon succeeds; two local instances complete a connect round-trip; lib tests still build without the daemon feature.
close_reason: Done: mjolnir-meshd builds with --features daemon; loopback round-trip verified (2.8ms, --no-relay --bind 127.0.0.1:0); lib stays iroh-free without the feature (65 lib tests pass). Deploy Dockerfile/build.sh updated to pass --features daemon.

title: Headless iroh-transport router daemon (mjolnir-meshd)
description: The only [[bin]] today is 'mjolnir-mesh' built from crates/mjolnir-node — the AUDIO example client (pulls mjolnir-audio -> cpal/ALSA + tract-onnx). Wrong thing to deploy on a headless router: needs audio HW, heavy, hard to static-cross-compile. Need a headless daemon binary for the substrate (crates/mjolnir-mesh: alloc/babel/crdt/tun). OPEN DESIGN QUESTION: substrate-only, or also include the iroh QUIC transport (currently only in mjolnir-node/mjolnir-moq, not in mjolnir-mesh deps)? This binary is the prerequisite for any router container deploy.
design: SCOPE RESOLVED: substrate + iroh transport, iroh-first, NO audio. Router = transport-only ('shuttle packets'). Substrate (crates/mjolnir-mesh) is transport-agnostic via DatagramConn trait seam; reuse iroh wiring pattern from mjolnir-node/mesh.rs (Endpoint + IROH_SECRET + MemoryLookup + ticket bootstrap), drop audio/room. MISSING GLUE: (1) production DatagramConn impl over iroh::Connection (only test MockConn exists); (2) PeerInterface::create drops TUN device handle (let _device) - must expose read/write halves for spawn_encap_pair. PHASES: P0 iroh endpoint + QUIC connectivity between two nodes (NO TUN - de-risks iroh-in-container before TUN-in-container risk). P1 IrohDatagramConn + single TUN /31 link via spawn_encap_pair (ping across mesh). P2 multi-peer full mesh + subnet alloc + CRDT gossip. P3 babeld supervision for cross-site routing. MTU: keep TUN MTU ~1300 (under QUIC datagram limit, else EncapError::DatagramTooLarge).
notes: Scope decided 2026-06-18: audio is a client-layer concern (mjolnir-node), router only carries packets. See memory project_router_role_decision.
close_reason: Complete and deployed fleet-wide. All design phases shipped: P0 iroh endpoint + id/listen/connect (02g); P1 production IrohDatagramConn over iroh::Connection + per-peer TUN via spawn_tunnel (both MISSING GLUE items implemented in mjolnir-meshd.rs); P2 multi-peer + subnet alloc + CRDT gossip (23a/kui/chn/k8c — convergence field-validated 2026-07-02, anti-entropy count=4 on all nodes); P3 babeld supervision (83k, m8t procd split, nrr reap hardening). Static aarch64 musl binary cross-built (deploy/openwrt/build.sh), running on all 4 OpenWrt routers via the health-gated apply pipeline, carrying validated client traffic (apo). Scope held: transport-only, no audio. Unblocks ut9 (RouterOS OCI image).

title: US-002: Subnet claim FWW merge + conflict resolution
description:
Implement first-writer-wins merge for `/subnets/{cidr}` entries. Mirrors the existing LeaseEntry FWW logic but keyed by CIDR rather than IP.

## Behavior
- On receiving a `SubnetClaimUpdate`, compare incoming `claimed_at` HLC against any local entry for the same CIDR.
- If incoming HLC is strictly lower → incoming wins; replace local entry; emit a conflict event so the losing local owner can rewrite its claim and reconfigure babeld.
- If incoming HLC is strictly higher → discard.
- If equal → tie-break on `owner_node_id` lexicographic.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pure `resolve_subnet_conflict(&a, &b)` returns (winner, loser) deterministically
- [ ] CRDT store `put_subnet` returns `MergeResult::Inserted | Updated | Unchanged | Conflict { winner, loser }`
- [ ] Unit tests cover: same CIDR + different owners + different HLCs (both orderings), equal HLC tie-break, identical entries (Unchanged)
- [ ] cargo check --workspace passes
- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings passes
- [ ] cargo test --workspace passes

title: US-003: Subnet allocation — derive preferred /24, check CRDT, claim
description:
On first boot at a new site (no local peers detected within the 10s detection window), the daemon must pick a /24 from the mesh address space (default `10.42.0.0/16`) and claim it.

## Algorithm
1. Enumerate current `/subnets/{cidr}` entries from the CRDT.
2. Preferred /24 = `10.42.{ blake3(node_id) % 256 }.0/24`.
3. If preferred is already claimed, increment third octet until a free one is found.
4. Wait 10s claim cooldown; if a local peer appears in that window, abort and join Mode 1.
5. Write `/subnets/{cidr}` with current HLC and gossip.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pure `pick_subnet(node_id, claimed: &HashSet<IpNet>, base: IpNet)` returns deterministic preferred /24 + fallback walk
- [ ] Integration test with a mocked CRDT store: 3 routers, distinct node_ids, all pick non-overlapping /24s
- [ ] Cooldown path: if a `local_peer_detected` signal fires within 10s, no claim is written
- [ ] cargo check --workspace passes
- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings passes
- [ ] cargo test --workspace passes

title: US-001: CRDT data model — SubnetClaim, IpAddr-typed leases, gossip enum update
description:
Replace the placeholder routing types in the CRDT data model with the subnet-claim ledger and make IP fields version-agnostic.

## Changes
- Add `SubnetClaim { cidr: IpNet, owner_node_id: String, site_name: Option<String>, claimed_at: HLC }` (introduce `ipnet` crate dep).
- Remove any `RouteEntry` / `RouteUpdate` if present in code (none currently — greenfield).
- Change `LeaseEntry.ip: Ipv4Addr` → `LeaseEntry.ip: std::net::IpAddr`. Same for `DnsEntry.ip` and `ServiceEntry.ip`.
- Update `GossipMessage` enum: drop `RouteUpdate`; add `SubnetClaimUpdate { cidr: String, entry: SubnetClaim }` and `SubnetClaimRelease { cidr: String, hlc: HLC }`.
- Postcard round-trip tests for all updated/new types.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `SubnetClaim` struct defined with the four fields above
- [ ] `LeaseEntry.ip`, `DnsEntry.ip`, `ServiceEntry.ip` are all `IpAddr`
- [ ] `GossipMessage` enum reflects the new variants (no `RouteUpdate`)
- [ ] Postcard serialize/deserialize round-trips covered by unit tests for each type
- [ ] cargo check --workspace passes
- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings passes
- [ ] cargo test --workspace passes

title: Wire /users CRDT record into meshd + fleet-validate propagation
description: Follow-on from spike 2xd (validated at lib level): wire GossipMessage::UserUpdate into the production daemon so a /users record converges mesh-wide over the live 802.11s gossip layer. Adds: UserBook store (users.state, tolerant load + tmp/rename persist), apply_user_message in the dispatch loop (merge_user LWW + persist + log), full-map re-broadcast in anti_entropy_loop (announce_user_book), and a plaintext users.seed injection path (username:Display lines, fresh-HLC stamped, registered_by=self) standing in for the p6u identity-submission control plane. Validate by seeding a user on one node and observing convergence (users.state / logread) on another. Advances bc7/p6u.
design: FLEET-VALIDATED 2026-07-04 on 0393686-dirty (4 nodes: wr3000s-a, m3000-b, m3000, tr3000). Seeded ada:Ada Lovelace via /etc/mjolnir/users.seed on m3000-b (node id 2b26d886...). Converged to all 3 other nodes in ~12s over 802.11s (well under the 20s anti-entropy tick): each logged 'gossip: received user record user=ada display=Ada Lovelace by=2b26d886...', users.state grew 1->160 bytes identically fleet-wide (persisted). LWW UPDATE also validated: edited seed to 'Ada, Countess of Lovelace', propagated to tr3000 in ~30s. registered_by correctly carries the originating node id everywhere. Proves the full path on real hardware: seed -> merge_user LWW -> gossip -> apply -> persist -> observable. Seed removed post-validation (stops re-origination; persisted record remains until a tombstone/removal path exists — future work). Next: p6u proper (ingest from pending/ spool instead of the users.seed stand-in) + a read API (bc7).
close_reason: Closed

title: Field-validate /users + service gossip on the physical 802.11s fleet
description: The spike (2xd) proved /users convergence over an in-process transport; this proves it on real hardware — the follow-up 0yb got for the address book. On the deployed OpenWrt 802.11s fleet: a UserRecord (and a ServiceEntry) written on node A converges to node B over the real radio backhaul within the target latency; survives node churn; anti-entropy heals a missed update. AC: cross-node /users + service convergence observed on >=2 physical nodes over 802.11s, offline. [test_tier: thorough] Depends on the daemon-wiring stories (zhg, p6u, S2).

title: daemon feature build broken: tun::spawn_overlay_tun import (renamed to spawn_overlay)
description: CORRECTED DIAGNOSIS 2026-07-04: NOT a rename. spawn_overlay_tun still exists (tun/iface.rs:174) and is exported (tun/mod.rs:14), but both are #[cfg(target_os="linux")]. spawn_overlay (overlay.rs:127) is a DIFFERENT fn with a different signature — do NOT swap the import to it (would break the Linux build). Real cause: meshd.rs:33 imports spawn_overlay_tun UNCONDITIONALLY, but it's Linux-only, so 'cargo build -p mjolnir-mesh --features daemon' fails E0432 on macOS (darwin) hosts. The daemon is a Linux/OpenWrt target (aarch64-unknown-linux-musl via deploy/openwrt/build.sh) and builds fine on Linux — the field fleet runs it. So this is a macOS-dev-ergonomics papercut, not a deploy-target regression, and NOT a simple import fix (meshd has deep Linux-only deps: rtnetlink, tun — it cannot build on macOS regardless of this import). Resolution options: (a) wontfix/by-design + doc note 'build the daemon on Linux or via the cross script'; (b) if native macOS 'cargo check --features daemon' is wanted for dev, it's a larger cfg-gating effort across meshd, not a one-liner. Recommend (a).
notes: WONTFIX 2026-07-04 (by design): mjolnir-meshd is a Linux/OpenWrt target (rtnetlink + tun deps); it does not build natively on macOS and is not meant to. Build it on Linux or via deploy/openwrt/build.sh. Not a rename regression. Doc-note candidate only.
